my_sentence: I can stay the company 1h instead.
correction: I can stay in the company for an hour instead.
explanation: 'Stay' needs the preposition 'in' before a place ('stay in the company'), and a duration takes 'for' ('for an hour'). Also say 'an hour', not the figure '1h', in speech.
correct_examples: I can stay in the office for an hour after work.
She stayed at home for two days.
context: Flexible working hours — making up time by staying late

my_sentence: It not depend on your gender.
correction: It doesn't depend on your gender.
explanation: A negative present-simple statement needs the auxiliary 'do' plus 'not', and with a third-person singular subject it is 'doesn't depend' (not 'not depend').
correct_examples: It doesn't depend on the weather.
The price doesn't change very often.
context: Whether working hours differ by gender

my_sentence: The company need to pay a lot of money for him.
correction: The company needs to pay a lot of money for him.
explanation: A singular subject ('the company') takes the third-person singular verb 'needs' in the present simple.
correct_examples: The company needs to hire more staff.
Each employee needs a badge to enter.
context: A worker injured in a dangerous factory

my_sentence: Some people who work in the office, it's the safe job.
correction: Working in an office is the safest job.
explanation: To say something ranks highest among all options, use the superlative 'the safest', not 'the safe job'. The definite article 'the' signals a superlative.
correct_examples: This is the safest option of all.
It's the easiest job in the company.
context: Which jobs are the most comfortable / safe

my_sentence: Many people will try to apply it.
correction: Many people will try to apply for it.
explanation: You 'apply for' a job or position (and 'apply to be' something), so the verb needs the preposition 'for'; 'apply it' means something different (put it into use). (assistant note)
correct_examples: Hundreds of people applied for the job.
She applied to be a volunteer.

explanation: A singular countable noun ('thing') needs an article; here use the indefinite 'a' — 'a good thing'.
correct_examples: That's a good thing.
It was a bad idea.
context: Discussing the cons of the legal system

my_sentence: You can easy find evidence to protect yourself.
correction: You can easily find evidence to protect yourself.
explanation: Use the adverb 'easily' to modify the verb 'find', not the adjective 'easy'. (This is a recurring weak spot.)
correct_examples: You can easily check it online.
He easily solved the problem.
context: CCTV cameras make it easy to find evidence

my_sentence: For me, I'm interest in my job.
correction: For me, I'm interested in my job.
explanation: To describe how you feel, use the -ed participle adjective 'interested in', not the noun/verb 'interest'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I'm interested in history.
She's very interested in the project.
context: The benefit of liking your own job

my_sentence: Their salary is lower at the beginning.
correction: Their salaries are lower in the beginning.
explanation: The fixed time phrase is 'in the beginning' (= at first), not 'at the beginning' here; and a plural subject ('their salaries') takes 'are'.
correct_examples: In the beginning, the pay is quite low.
Salaries are usually lower in the beginning.
context: Doctors earn little when they start out

my_sentence: When they become older, their salary become more.
correction: When they get older, their salary becomes higher.
explanation: The singular subject 'their salary' takes 'becomes'; and salaries 'get/become higher', not 'become more'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: As she gets older, her salary becomes higher.
The work becomes easier with practice.
context: Doctors' pay rising with age and experience

my_sentence: Having high salary means you can buy anything that you want.
correction: Having a high salary means you can buy anything you want.
explanation: The singular countable noun phrase 'high salary' needs the article 'a'. ('that' is also optional in the object relative clause.) (assistant note)
correct_examples: Having a high salary gives you more freedom.
She wants a good job with a high salary.
context: The pros of a high salary

my_sentence: If they want to get higher salaries, they have to lose their time.
correction: If they want to get higher salaries, they have to sacrifice their time.
explanation: Giving up your time deliberately for a goal is 'sacrifice your time', not 'lose your time' (which sounds accidental/wasteful).
correct_examples: They sacrifice their free time to earn more.
He sacrificed his weekends to finish the project.
context: The cons of chasing a high salary

my_sentence: They don't have some time to take care of their families.
correction: They don't have any time to take care of their families.
explanation: In a negative sentence, use 'any', not 'some': 'don't have any time'. ('some' is for affirmatives/offers.) (assistant note)
correct_examples: I don't have any money left.
There isn't any time to waste.
context: No time for family when working too much

my_sentence: You can use your power to let them drink a lot of alcohol even though they don't like it.
correction: You can use your power to make them drink a lot of alcohol even though they don't like it.
explanation: 'Make someone do something' means to force/compel them; 'let someone do something' means to allow them. Since they don't want to, the right causative is 'make'. (assistant note)
correct_examples: The boss made them stay late.
Don't make me repeat myself.
context: A senior person pressuring juniors to drink at work
